coredata.dc:title: Fracture toughness and high temperature strength of unidirectionally solidified Nb–Si binary and Nb–Ti–Si ternary alloys
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Sekido, Nobuaki
Kimura, Yoshisato
Miura, Seiji
Wei, Fu-Gao
Mishima, Yoshinao
coredata.dc:description: The (Nb)/Nb3Si eutectic alloys in the Nb–Si binary and Nb–Ti–Si ternary systems were unidirectionally solidified in an optical floating zone melting furnace. The effect of solidification rates on microstructures of the alloys is investigated. The coupled growth of (Nb) and Nb3Si is observed in the binary alloy solidified at 10mm/h. The ternary alloys exhibit the development of discontinuous (Nb) particles within the Nb3Si matrix. The unidirectionally solidified alloys show improved fracture toughness and enhanced high temperature compressive strength as compared with the arc-melted counterparts. The role of an aligned eutectic microstructure in toughening is discussed from the existing models based on the crack bridging and the crack deflection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Intermetallics
Unidirectional solidification
Scanning electron microscopy
Strain

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Microstructures of the unidirectionally solidified alloys
Fracture toughness and crack propagation behavior

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Longitudinal (a–d) and transverse (e and f) sections of the microstructures for the unidirectionally solidified alloys: (a and e) Nb–17.5Si at 10mm/h, (b and f) Nb–17.5Si at 100mm/h, (c and g) Nb–10Ti–17.5Si at 10mm/h, and (d and h) Nb–10Ti–17.5Si at 100mm/h. The arrows indicate the solidification direction.
Microstructures of the unidirectionally solidified alloys annealed at 1773K for 500h: (a) Nb–17.5Si at 10mm/h and (b) Nb–10Ti–17.5Si at 10mm/h.
Fractograph of the as-unidirectionally-solidified Nb–17.5Si alloy after the bending test.
Crack propagation behavior of the as-unidirectionally-solidified alloys: (a) Nb–17.5Si and (b) Nb–10Ti–17.5Si. The arrows indicate the solidification direction.

Stress–strain curves obtained by the compression tests at 1273, 1473, and 1673K for the unidirectionally solidified alloys: (a) Nb–17.5Si solidified at 10mm/h, (b) Nb–10Ti–17.5Si solidified at 10mm/h and annealed at 1673K for 500h, and (c) Nb–10Ti–17.5Si solidified at 100mm/h and annealed at 1673K for 500h.
Temperature dependence of the compressive 0.2% flow stress of the present alloys. The strengths of CMSX-10 [44], Nb–10Ti–20Si [27], and DS Nb–Ti–Cr–Al–Si [18] are shown for comparison.
Fracture toughness, K Q, evaluated by the chevron-notch fracture toughness measurements
The physical property data used for the calculation

article.ce:title: Fracture toughness and high temperature strength of unidirectionally solidified Nb–Si binary and Nb–Ti–Si ternary alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Tokyo Institute of Technology, Yokohama 226-8502, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Materials Science and Engineering, Hokkaido University, Sapporo 060-0813, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Steel Research Center, National Institute for Materials Science, 1-2-1 Sengen, Tsukuba 305-0047, Japan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Wisconsin-Madison, Madison, WI 53706, USA. Tel.: +1 608 265 4986.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The (Nb)/Nb3Si eutectic alloys in the Nb–Si binary and Nb–Ti–Si ternary systems were unidirectionally solidified in an optical floating zone melting furnace. The effect of solidification rates on microstructures of the alloys is investigated. The coupled growth of (Nb) and Nb3Si is observed in the binary alloy solidified at 10mm/h. The ternary alloys exhibit the development of discontinuous (Nb) particles within the Nb3Si matrix. The unidirectionally solidified alloys show improved fracture toughness and enhanced high temperature compressive strength as compared with the arc-melted counterparts. The role of an aligned eutectic microstructure in toughening is discussed from the existing models based on the crack bridging and the crack deflection.

ce:section.ce:para: The third generation single crystal Ni-based superalloys are used as turbine engine airfoils exposed to a high temperature environment slightly over 1400K. However further increases in the temperature capability of the superalloys seem to be difficult, since the operating temperature is approaching to 90% of their melting temperatures. Therefore the performance improvement of aircraft engines will necessitate the development of a new class of heat resistant materials.

Last decades alloys based on Nb–silicides have received considerable attention for potential high temperature application [1,2]. According to the Nb–Si binary phase diagram [3], a (Nb)/Nb5Si3 two-phase microstructure forms through a eutectic reaction (L→(Nb)+Nb3Si) at 2193K, and a eutectoid reaction (Nb3Si→(Nb)+Nb5Si3) at 2043K. It has been demonstrated that (Nb)/Nb5Si3 two-phase alloys in the Nb–Si binary system exhibit moderate fracture toughness and reasonable high temperature strength [4–6]. However, due to the catastrophic oxidation behavior of Nb-rich Nb–Si binary alloys at high temperatures [7], the recent studies on Nb–Si alloys have evolved into multi-component systems to strike a balance between mechanical properties and environmental resistance [2,8,9]. The additions of Al and Cr lead to improved oxidation resistance [7,10], but increased DBTT of the (Nb) phase [11,12]. The additions of Mo and W enhance high temperature compressive strength of the alloys [13–16]. The Ti addition to the Nb–Si binary alloys improves both room temperature toughness and oxidation resistance [17–20]. Hf plays an important role in the enhancement of high temperature strength, room temperature toughness and oxidation resistance of the Nb–silicide based alloys [21,22].

At the same time, the toughness improvement through microstructure control has been attempted in eutectic or hyper-eutectic Nb–Si alloys that are expected to show lower damage tolerance but better oxidation resistance and high temperature strength than Nb-rich Nb–Si alloys [23–27]. The development of a (Nb)/Nb5Si3 lamellar microstructure through Nb3Si decomposition has been found to be effective in improving the toughness of the alloys [24,25,27]. However, alloys with fully lamellar microstructure are still brittle at room temperature, and thus, the alloy design to develop a “duplex” microstructure, where coarse (Nb) particles co-exist with the (Nb)/Nb5Si3 lamellae, is necessary to provide some compressive ductility at room temperature for the alloys [24]. This fact suggests that relatively coarse (Nb) particles formed from liquid plays a significant role in the toughening of the alloys. These results stimulate us to control the (Nb)/Nb3Si eutectic solidification, since the microstructure with columnar ductile phase aligning directionally within a brittle matrix can provide better opportunity for the occurrence of toughening mechanisms, such as the crack bridging toughening by the ductile phase [28–30].

The focal point in the present study is the control of the (Nb)/Nb3Si eutectic solidification by means of a unidirectional solidification technique in the Nb–Si binary and Nb–Ti–Si ternary systems. Alloys were unidirectionally solidified in an optical floating zone melting furnace with variable rates of solidification. The microstructure control through Nb3Si decomposition was attempted simultaneously. The effect of microstructural variables on the fracture toughness and high temperature compressive strength was investigated. The toughening mechanisms that operate in the unidirectionally solidified alloys are discussed from the existing toughening models based on the crack bridging and the crack deflection.

Rod ingots with the dimensions of 11mm in diameter and 70mm in length were prepared by arc-melting of high purity Nb, Ti, and Si under an Ar atmosphere. The nominal alloy compositions were Nb–17.5at.% Si and Nb–10at.% Ti–17.5at.% Si. The alloys were unidirectionally solidified in an optical floating zone (OFZ) furnace at the rates of 10 and 100mm/h under an Ar gas flowing atmosphere. Due to the sluggish diffusion, the high temperature phase Nb3Si was retained at room temperature after unidirectional solidification. It is reported that the nose temperatures of the TTT curves for Nb3Si decomposition are 1773K for the Nb–Si binary alloys [31], and 1673K for the Nb–Ti–Si ternary alloys [27]. Therefore, the binary alloys are annealed at 1773K, and the ternary alloys at 1673K for 500h under vacuum after unidirectional solidification. To minimize oxidation during annealing, alloys were wrapped with Nb foils prior to annealing.

Microstructural observations were made by a scanning electron microscope (SEM). The constituent phases of the alloys were identified by X-ray diffractometry (XRD), and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). Mechanical properties of the alloys were evaluated by fracture toughness measurements and compression tests. Fracture toughness was determined by four-point bending tests (outer span: 21mm, inner span: 7mm) using chevron-notched specimens, and analytical calculation using the Bluhm's slice model [32]. The dimensions of bending test specimen were 5mm×5mm×30mm, and the chevron notch was introduced perpendicular to the solidification direction using electro-discharge machining (EDM) to set up the initial ligament length being 0.8 against the width of the bending bar. The tests were carried out using an Instron type testing machine under the cross-head speed of 8.3×10−7 mm/s. Compression tests were conducted on rectangular specimens at temperatures up to 1673K with a nominal strain rate of 1.0×10−4 s−1. The dimensions of the compression test specimen were 2mm×2mm×5mm, and the loading direction was parallel to the solidification direction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructures of the unidirectionally solidified alloys
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the microstructures of the Nb–17.5Si and Nb–10Ti–17.5Si alloys unidirectionally solidified at 10 and 100mm/h. The high temperature phase Nb3Si is retained at room temperature in the as-unidirectionally-solidified alloys. The Nb–17.5Si alloy solidified at 10mm/h shows the development of (Nb) plates within the Nb3Si matrix, which indicates that the coupled growth of the two phases is attained at this solidification rate. On the other hand, the Nb–17.5Si alloy solidified at 100mm/h exhibits the formation of discontinuous (Nb) particles within the Nb3Si matrix. The formation of discontinuous (Nb) particles implies that the growth velocity of Nb3Si is faster than that of (Nb) at the given undercooling, so that the (Nb) particles are cut off repeatedly from access to liquid during solidification. Namely, the coupled growth of the two phases is attained at 10mm/h, because the solidification rate is slow enough to inhibit the precedent growth of the Nb3Si phase. For the Nb–10Ti–17.5Si alloys, the formation of cellular eutectic microstructures, where discontinuous (Nb) particles are dispersed within the Nb3Si matrix, is prevailing regardless of the solidification rates. Although the continuity of the growth of (Nb) seems to be improved by lowering the rate of solidification, the coupled growth of the two phases is not attained under the present experimental condition. Since the precipitation of (Nb) and Nb3Si from liquid is not an invariant reaction in the Nb–10Ti–17.5Si ternary alloy, there is a freezing range upon solidification, in which solid and liquid phases can co-exist in equilibrium. Owing to the presence of a freezing range, it is difficult for the solid/liquid interface to maintain the plane-front configuration [33]. Therefore, a steeper thermal gradient in the liquid phase and a lower solidification rate than the present experimental condition would be required to attain the coupled growth of (Nb) and Nb3Si in the ternary alloys.

Fig. 2 shows the microstructures of the annealed alloys. For the Nb–10Ti–17.5Si alloys, the high temperature phase Nb3Si has completely transformed into the (Nb) and Nb5Si3 phases with a lamellar microstructure. However, contrary to the previous reports [27,31], the decomposition of Nb3Si is rarely observed in the unidirectionally solidified Nb–17.5Si alloys even after the annealing at 1773K for 500h. The reason for the extremely slow kinetics of the decomposition reaction is yet known, but the reduction of impurities by the zone refining effect of unidirectional solidification could be responsible. It has been demonstrated that the kinetics of Nb3Si decomposition is quite sensitive to alloying additions; the additions of Ti and Zr to the binary alloys significantly accelerate the Nb3Si decomposition kinetics [23,27]. The decomposition kinetic can be enhanced by the inclusions of impurities [34]. Further investigations are necessary to understand the Nb3Si decomposition mechanisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fracture toughness and crack propagation behavior
ce:section.ce:para: The chevron-notch specimen is useful for measuring the plane-strain fracture toughness of brittle materials, since a sharp natural crack is produced at the early stage of loading, so that no pre-cracking is required [32]. Due to the notch configuration, however, the chevron-notch specimen is disadvantageous to observe crack propagation behavior during bending tests. Therefore in the present study, the chevron-notched specimens are used for toughness evaluation, and the straight through notched specimens are used for crack observations. For the Nb–17.5Si alloys, the annealed alloys are not subjected to the mechanical tests, since the decomposition of the high temperature phase Nb3Si has not completed after the annealing at 1773K for 500h.

Table 1 summarizes the fracture toughness of the alloys determined by the chevron-notch fracture toughness measurements. The arc-cast Nb–18.7Si alloy shows lower fracture toughness than the arc-cast Nb–25Si alloy in spite of its higher volume fraction of the (Nb) phase. The same tendency has been reported by Bewlay et al. [4]. These results imply that, in the arc-cast alloys with the eutectic or hyper-eutectic compositions, toughening mechanisms other than crack bridging are also contributing to the work of fracture. The Ti doped ternary alloys generally exhibit higher fracture toughness than the binary alloys. The unidirectionally solidified alloys show higher fracture toughness than the arc-melted counterparts. In addition, the toughness value increases with decreasing the rate of unidirectional solidification. By comparison with the toughness of the unidirectionally solidified Nb–10Ti–17.5Si alloys before and after annealing, the annealed alloys obviously show higher fracture toughness than the as-unidirectionally-solidified alloys. This result clearly indicates that the development of the (Nb)/Nb5Si3 lamellar microstructure through the Nb3Si decomposition is effective in enhancing the toughness of the alloys.

The present study reveals that the control of (Nb)/Nb3Si eutectic solidification by unidirectional solidification and the development of a (Nb)/Nb5Si3 lamellar microstructure by Nb3Si decomposition are both effective in improving the toughness of the alloys. Hereafter, the effect of an aligned eutectic microstructure on toughening is evaluated by the Ashby model [35]. To extract the role of unidirectional solidification, only as-unidirectionally-solidified alloys are considered. The toughness increment, ΔK
C , from the crack bridging of a ductile particle is given by the following equation:
(1)
Δ
K C
= E
C
V f
σ 0
E
a 0
where E, V f, σ 0, and a 0 are the Young's modulus, volume fraction, yield strength, and radius of the ductile phase particle, respectively. The parameter C is a material constant that represents the degree of constraint imposed upon a ductile particle from the matrix, and is 1.6 when the ductile particle plastically deforms and fails into chisel point without interface decohesion. Table 2 summarizes the physical data used for the calculations. The parameter C is taken as 1.6; since the fractographs after bending tests have indicated that the (Nb) particles fail in ductile manner with small amounts of interface debonding, as shown in Fig. 3 . The Young's modulus of the (Nb) phase is taken from literature [6]. The average radii of the (Nb) particles in the Nb–17.5Si and Nb–10Ti–17.5Si alloys are determined as 5.0 and 14μm, respectively [36,37]. The yield strength of the (Nb) phase is estimated from the Vickers hardness of the Nb solid solution phase [24] using the following equation [38]:
(2)
σ = 2.4 Hv
( MPa )
where Hv is the Vickers hardness value. For the as-unidirectionally-solidified Nb–17.5Si alloy, the calculated toughness increment, 13.8MPam1/2, reasonably agrees with the experimental value of 14.5MPam1/2, considering that the fracture toughness of the silicide phase is reported as 1–3MPam1/2 [7,39]. However, the calculated toughness increment, 20.8MPam1/2, of the as-unidirectionally-solidified Nb–10Ti–17.5Si alloy is appreciably higher than that experimentally determined. The deviation of the predicted value from the experimental value is resulted from the discontinuous characteristic (or low aspect ratio along the solidification direction) of the (Nb) phase in the ternary alloys. Fig. 4 shows the crack propagation behavior of the Nb–17.5Si and Nb–10Ti–17.5Si alloys. For the Nb–17.5Si alloy, the main crack generated from the notch root propagates straightly, and the occurrence of the crack bridging by (Nb) rods is evident. Many microcracks are also observed near the main crack, where (Nb) rods effectively prohibit further microcracking. For the Nb–10Ti–17.5Si alloy, on the other hand, the main crack tends to deflect at (Nb) particles, and propagates preferentially within the brittle Nb3Si matrix. This fact indicates that the toughening from the crack bridging of (Nb) is larger than that from the crack deflection, and the development of (Nb) particles with higher aspect ratios can provide a better opportunity for the (Nb) particles to contribute to crack bridging toughening.

We have demonstrated that the continuity (or high aspect ratio) of (Nb) particle is one of the important parameters that determine the toughness of the alloys. However, the unidirectionally solidified ternary alloys show higher toughness than the binary alloys, though the ternary alloys show less continuity (or lower aspect ratios) for (Nb) particles. The reason for the toughness improvement by the Ti addition is the increase in the size and ductility of the (Nb) phase. Microstructure observations clearly depict that the size of (Nb) particles is increased by the Ti addition. As is indicated in Eq. (1), a larger sized ductile particle can provide an alloy with higher toughness increment, wherein the crack bridging mechanism is operative. In addition, it has been shown that the addition of Ti enhances the ductility of bcc solid solutions in the Nb–Ti–Cr [11,40–42], Nb–Ti–Al [43], Nb–Ti [20], and Nb–Ti–Si [17,24] systems. Namely, the number of (Nb) particles that contribute to the crack bridging toughening would be comparably smaller in the ternary alloys than in the binary alloys, but once the (Nb) particles bridged a crack, larger amount of energy absorption can be expected from the plastic deformation of the (Nb) particles.

Fig. 5 shows the stress–strain curves obtained by compression tests at 1273, 1473, and 1673K. The alloys show some compressive deformability over 1473K. After 10% of compression at 1673K, some cracks were formed in the binary alloy, while no visible cracks were observed on the surface of the ternary alloys. In the present study, the ternary alloys are annealed before the compression tests, and therefore, the high temperature phase Nb3Si has decomposed into the (Nb)/Nb3Si lamellar microstructure. In addition, the mobility of dislocations in the (Nb) phase is enhanced by Ti additions [41]. The higher volume fraction of the (Nb) phase, as well as the increased dislocation mobility in the (Nb) phase provide better deformability for the ternary alloys. It should be noted that the yield drop is typically observed upon high temperature compression. The reasons for the yield drop are yet known, but the increase in the density of mobile dislocations, especially in the silicide phases, during deformation may be responsible.

The temperature dependence of the compressive 0.2% flow stress for the present alloys is summarized in Fig. 6 . The unidirectionally solidified Nb–17.5Si alloy shows excellent strength: 580MPa at 1673K. The microstructure of the as-unidirectionally-solidified Nb–17.5Si alloy is characterized by the development of continuous (Nb) plates within the Nb3Si matrix. Since most of the interfaces are aligned parallel to the loading direction, the alloy would be more resistant to interfacial sliding during high temperature deformation. Another possible reason for the excellent high temperature strength of the Nb–17.5Si alloy is the development of a texture upon unidirectional solidification. It has been found that the Nb3Si matrix growths preferentially toward the [001] direction, and thereby a [001] texture develops along the solidification direction in the Nb–17.5Si alloy [36]. The loading direction of the compression tests in the present study is parallel to the solidification direction. Since the shortest translation vector of Nb3Si is [001], the development of a [001] texture could provide high resistance for slip deformation involving the Burgers vector of [001]. The analysis for the slip systems and the defect structures of the Nb3Si phase is in progress.

The ternary alloys show lower strength than the binary alloys for the full range of test temperatures because of the higher volume fraction of the (Nb) phase, and the increased dislocation mobility in the (Nb) phase. The unidirectionally solidified Nb–10Ti–17.5Si alloys show higher compressive strength than the arc-melted Nb–10Ti–20Si alloys [27]. Moreover the high temperature strength is enhanced by lowering the rate of unidirectional solidification. It is concluded that a unidirectional solidification technique, especially at a low solidification rate, is effective in enhancing the strength of the alloys. The evaluation of creep strength is planed for future work.

The (Nb)/Nb3Si eutectic solidification is controlled in the Nb–Si binary and Nb–Si–Ti ternary alloys by means of unidirectional solidification. The slower solidification rates yield the coarser microstructures, as well as the better continuity in the (Nb) growth toward the solidification direction. The decomposition of Nb3Si is kinetically much slower in the unidirectionally solidified alloys than in the arc-melted alloys; probably because impurities are removed by the zone refining effect of unidirectional solidification. The development of continuous (Nb) rods provides a better opportunity for the (Nb) phase to contribute to the crack bridging toughening, and thereby, higher toughness improvement. The development of a (Nb)/Nb5Si3 lamellar microstructure further improves the toughness of the alloys. The high temperature compressive strength is also enhanced by the application of a unidirectional solidification technique to the present alloys. The fracture toughness of 14.5MPam1/2 at room temperature, and the compressive strength of 580MPa at 1673K are achieved by a suitable microstructure control in a Nb–17.5at.% Si alloy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Prof. M. Takeyama and Dr. Y. Yamamoto (Tokyo Inst. Tech.) for technical supports and fruitful comments on the unidirectional solidification experiment.

Evolution of Refractory Metals and Alloys
Refractory Metals: Extraction, Processing and Applications
Refractory Metals: Extraction, Processing and Applications
Proceedings of the Third International Symposium on Structural Intermetallics

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: S. Miura, Hokkaido University, unpublished data.
N. Sekido, Y. Kimura, S. Miura, Y. Mishima, in preparation.
N. Sekido, Doctoral Dissertation, Tokyo Institute of Technology, 2002.
